Multi-Model Machine Learning Approaches for
Fault and Anomaly Detection in Petroleum Product
Distribution: A Comparison

Abstract—Maintaining the integrity of petroleum transporta-
tion is essential for operational safety and efficiency. Substantial
issues arise from faults in fuel transportation systems, including
distribution valve malfunctions and anomalies in transit patterns,
such as theft or unexpected fuel losses. This work presents
a machine learning approach that combines supervised fault
detection models, like Logistic Regression, SVM, Random Forest,
and XGBoost, with unsupervised anomaly detection methods,
such as k-means, Isolation Forest, and Autoencoders. The study
employs real fuel transportation data and anomaly detection
datasets obtained from petroleum distribution networks. Experi-
mental results show that combining supervised and unsupervised
methods leads to better detection accuracy and reliability, making
the petroleum delivery system more dependable.
Index Terms—Fault detection, anomaly detection, petroleum
transportation, machine learning, predictive maintenance.
I. INTRODUCTION
The petroleum sector is a system that needs to have a
constant fuel supply. However, several problems related to the
operation of the company may negatively affect efficiency,
safety, and profitability. Deficiencies in fuel transportation
systems lead to fuel mixing, pollution and financial losses.
Environmental pollution can occur by leakages, spills and
improper handling of the product during transportation leading
to contamination of soil and water. These risks pose significant
ecological and economic concerns, hence effective risk assess-
ment and mitigation techniques are required [1]. Operational
anormalities in distribution networks include unexpected fuel
losses, fraudulent activities, and route variations, suggesting
possible inefficiencies or security breaches. A significant vul-
nerability in fuel distribution networks results from malfunc-
tions in valve devices that divide various fuel types in transport
trailers. Defects in these systems may lead to the wrong type
of fuel being used in vehicles, which leads to poor fuel quality,
reduced vehicle performance, and higher costs to both the
distributors and consumers of the fuel [2]. In addition, other
operational irregularities, including, unforeseen fuel losses,
fraudulent activities, and unauthorized route diversion may
point to poor management, security breaches or even fraud
within the value chain. Some examples of operational irregu-
larities include: fuel losses, fraud, and changes in the channel,
which are unexpected fuel losses, fraudulent actions, and
changes in the route. These anomalies may be an indication
of poor management or security breaches.
Research has investigated various methods to enhance fuel
distribution efficiency to address current challenges. Meta-
heuristic approaches have been applied to fuel distribution
networks for vehicle routing optimization under capacity con-
straints according to researches [3]. As these methods boost
logistical performance they are generally created for route op-
timization and scheduling needs rather than the identification
of mechanical problems or system issues. The research deliv-
ers a multi-model machine learning framework that integrates
both supervised models to detect faults and unsupervised
models to detect anomalies in petroleum distribution to resolve
these challenges. The main goal is to boost operational safety,
enhance maintenance strategies and complement traditional
fuel routing optimization methods.
Despite the fact that these conventional approaches are able
to identify obvious errors, they are not effective in identifying
complex and evolving patterns that, if ignored, could lead to
severe challenges. Because of this limitation, the diagnosis
of a malfunction is usually delayed, which results in prolong
periods of downtime and increased operational hazards.
This research examines various machine learning methods
for defect detection and anomaly recognition in petroleum
distribution systems. It integrates supervised and unsupervised

learning methodologies to deliver a comprehensive analysis of
fault patterns and operational anomalies rather than concentrat-
ing entirely on route optimization or individual fault detection
models. The research examines the correlation between feature
engineering and model performance, and how domain specific
characteristics can enhance predictive accuracy and minimize
false positives
The structure of this paper is as follows: Section II presents
a review of fault detection and anomaly detection techniques
in industrial and fuel distribution systems, with a focus on
machine learning-based approaches. Section III outlines the
methodology, including a description of the dataset, prepro-
cessing steps, and details on the supervised and unsupervised
models employed for fault detection and anomaly identifi-
cation. Section IV discusses challenges encountered during
the study and talk about the results and evaluates model
performance using classification metrics such as accuracy,
precision, recall, and F1-score. Section V concludes the paper
by summarizing key contributions and highlighting potential
areas for future work.
II. RELATED WORKS
Most of the papers written related to fault detection and
anomaly detection refer to data-driven machine learning mod-
els compared to the traditional statistical and rule-based ap-
proaches used to spot real-time data anomalies and faulty
behaviors during the distribution of petroleum products.
A. Fault Detection in Industrial Systems
The application of machine learning (ML) for fault detection
in industrial systems is widespread to predict when mainte-
nance is required and to prevent unexpected system failures.
The application of data-driven machine learning models has
been found to be more reliable than traditional rule-based
methods, especially when operating under changing condi-
tions.
Patil et al. [4] state that the use of intelligent sensors c
enable real-time fault detection through ongoing monitoring
of system performance. However, there are still issues with
the development of models that are able to perform well
across a range of operating conditions. In addition, Bode et al.
[5] explore the use of machine learning techniques for fault
detection in energy systems and highlight the problems of poor
feature selection, limited labelled data and generic models to
enhance fault classification accuracy.
Vaish et al. [6] present an overview of the use of ML in
power system fault diagnosis and demonstrate how supervised
and unsupervised learning can enhance fault detection, clas-
sification and location. They also highlight the advantages of
real time data processing with the help of advanced monitoring
tools like PMUs to enhance the reliability of the system.
Nevertheless, there are problems regarding generalization,
data quality and robustness that are still relevant for ML
models. Future work should be directed at enhancing the
adaptability of the model and improving predictive mainte-
nance strategies across various industries.
A popular method for identifying faults in industrial sys-
tems, machine learning (ML) ensures predictive maintenance
and reduces unplanned failures. Since unpredictable operating
conditions are frequently a problem for traditional rule-based
approaches, data-driven machine learning models offer a more
dependable alternative.
B. Anomaly Detection in Fuel Distribution
Recent research has focused on machine learning techniques
for anomaly detection in petroleum transportation, explore
both supervised and unsupervised learning approaches to de-
tect network and operational anomalies.
Savic et al. [7] employed Variational Autoencoders (VAE)
and KMeans clustering to detect anomalies in cellular IoT used
for freight tracking, particularly for perishable and hazardous
materials.
Haji et al. [8] utilized KNN, Local Outlier Factor (LOF),
and SVM to identify physical anomalies in the transport of
petroleum and hazardous gases, emphasizing deviations in
parameters like pressure and temperature.
Hindy et al. [9] applied Random Forest and Gradient
Boosting (XGBoost) to detect network anomalies in IoT-based
logistics systems.
Salej & Trebar [10] analyzed Logistic Regression, SVM,
and Gradient Boosting for identifying operational anomalies
in petroleum and gas transport, focusing on the challenges
posed by edge computing constraints.
Other studies explored hybrid approaches, such as combin-
ing Autoencoders, KMeans, and Isolation Forest, to handle
real-time data anomalies in IoT-based transportation and haz-
ardous materials transportation.
III. METHODOLOGY
This section examines the use of different supervised and
unsupervised learning algorithms to detect abnormalities and
faults in industrial systems related to petroleum product distri-
bution in different locations. It offers a thorough explanation of
datasets, data engineering, preprocessing steps, and algorithm-
specific parameter tweaking.
A. Dataset and Preprocessing
Two separate datasets are used: fuel transportation logs and
detailed operational records for each affected truck, covering
data for normal operating days and mixing event days. This
historical viewpoint allows for a more thorough analysis of
pre-failure conditions, helping to identify early warning signals
that precede mixing events. Petroleum distribution anomaly
dataset, which captures irregular transportation patterns and
unexpected losses, contains 214,003 instances, with 32,840
corresponding to probable anomalies. This collection mostly
covers historical transport data, with the majority of records
dating from 2021 to 2024 and recording abnormalities ob-
served during fossil fuel distribution, mainly petroleum prod-
ucts. The dataset also comprises signals collected from various
sensors that monitor traffic activity.
The combination of these datasets offers a more robust
approach to recognizing and addressing abnormalities in the

D. Unsupervised and semi-supervised Models for Anomaly
Detection
1) k-Means: partitions the data into k groups based on
similarity and classifies the data into normal and anomalous
patterns by measuring the distance of each data point from its
assigned cluster centroid [13] [12]. The most common metric
used to calculate the distance between a data point and its
centroid is the Euclidean distance:

2) Isolation Forest: is a tree-based method that isolates
anomalies by creating partitions based on randomly chosen
features and split values [13]. The anomaly score is determined
by how quickly a data point is isolated, with anomalies being
isolated at a shallow depth in the tree. The score s(x) is
calculated as:

where E(h(x)) is the average path length of a point x across
all trees, and c(n) is a normalization constant that accounts
for the expected path length in a random partition.
3) Autoencoders: neural networks trained to recognize nor-
mal patterns and detect deviations are trained on normal data,
and anomalies are detected by the reconstruction error. Points
that are not well reconstructed exhibit high error, indicating
that they are different from the normal patterns learned by
the network [14] [15]. The Mean Squared Error (MSE) is
commonly used to compute the reconstruction error:

IV. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
The results presented hereafter show a comparative analysis
of various machine-learning models for anomaly detection and
fault analysis in petroleum distribution. The main metrics used
to define the model’s efficiency and performance are accuracy,
precision, recall, f1 score, and roc area under the curve, where
it is noticed that both supervised and unsupervised models
showed an average to high accuracy in detecting anomalous
and faulty cases.
The ROC AUC score, which measures the model’s ability
to distinguish between normal and anomalous cases, varied
across different models, with the highest value observed for
KMeans & Isolation Forest (0.93) and XGBoost (0.90), indi-
cating strong predictive power. Models like SVM (0.90) and
Random Forest (0.85) also demonstrated high discriminative
ability, while Logistic Regression (0.68) and VAE (0.73)
showed lower effectiveness in distinguishing anomalies.
Recall, a crucial metric in anomaly detection, which mea-
sures the ability of a model to correctly identify positive
instances out of all actual positives, was highest for KMeans
& IF (0.89) and XGBoost (0.80), ensuring that most anomalies
were successfully identified. However, Logistic Regression
(0.40) and SVM (0.40) had low recall, meaning many anoma-
lies were missed despite their high precision. This trade-
off highlights that models prioritizing high recall—such as
XGBoost and KNN & LOF (0.83)—are better suited for
minimizing false negatives, which is critical in fault detection.
Precision, a metric that measures how many detected
anomalies were actual anomalies, was highest for Logistic

Regression (0.83) and SVM (0.83), suggesting they were
conservative in flagging anomalies but at the cost of missing
several. On the other hand, VAE (0.37) had the lowest pre-
cision, indicating that many of its anomaly predictions were
false positives.
F1 Score, which balances precision and recall and indicates
a good trade-off between correctly spotting anomalies (recall)
and minimizing false positives, favored KMeans & IF (0.84)
as the most well-rounded model, followed by XGBoost (0.70)
and KNN & LOF (0.70).
In summary, KMeans & Isolation Forest emerged as the
best-performing model, achieving the highest accuracy (0.95),
recall (0.89), and ROC AUC score (0.93), making it the most
reliable for anomaly detection in petroleum distribution.
The KMeans and Isolation Forest hybrid approach are the
most effective in anomaly detection due to their complemen-
tary strengths. KMeans groups normal transportation patterns,
allowing for clear distinction between standard operations and
anomalies. It segments data into well-defined clusters, making
deviations easily identifiable. Isolation Forest, on the other
hand, excels at detecting rare and unexpected deviations by
isolating anomalies quickly. This combination makes it par-
ticularly suitable for identifying complex distribution irregu-
larities in petroleum distribution. The hybrid model can handle
high-dimensional data effectively, maintaining accuracy even
when dealing with multiple factors influencing transportation
patterns. It also offers scalability and flexibility, adapting to
changes in operational norms and working with unlabelled
data. The combination of KMeans and Isolation Forest ensures
generalization across datasets and high sensitivity to rare
events, enhancing anomaly detection performance.
XGBoost and KNN & LOF also performed well, offering
a good trade-off between recall and precision, while Logis-
tic Regression and VAE struggled with recall and precision
balance, making them less effective for anomaly detection.
A. Limitations and Challenges
This study also revealed certain limitations. The relatively
small dataset size constrained the models’ ability to general-
ize, especially in complex scenarios. Increasing the amount
of training data, particularly with more examples of faulty
instances, could enhance performance. Additionally, anomalies
in fuel transportation are highly context-dependent; factors
such as delivery importance and cargo urgency play a crucial
role in determining what qualifies as an anomaly. Automated
systems often lack this contextual understanding, making it
difficult to distinguish between normal and abnormal behav-
iors.
Another significant challenge was the lack of publicly
available datasets. Most research papers do not share their
datasets, making it difficult to compare the effectiveness of
different algorithms. Additionally, many studies inject artificial
anomalies without explaining their methodology, leading to
inconsistencies when benchmarking new models. Even when
datasets are available, every company has its own internal
policies that define abnormal behavior, and these policies can
change over time due to evolving technical regulations or per-
sonnel decisions. Additionally, we observed that while models
like Logistic Regression and SVM are easier to interpret, their
lower recall rates make them less suitable for applications
where missing a fault could have severe consequences. These
models often struggle to capture complex relationships in
data, particularly when anomalies are rare and subtle. Since
recall measures the ability to identify all relevant anomalies,
lower recall rates can lead to undetected faults, increasing the
risk of operational failures. In high-stakes scenarios like fuel
transportation, missing an anomaly could mean overlooking a
potential safety hazard or a security threat, which could have
severe financial and environmental consequences.
B. Analysis of Case Studies and Interpretation of Results
The results of this study highlight the potential of machine
learning models to enhance fault and anomaly detection in
fuel transportation systems. Among the models evaluated,
XGBoost showed the greatest promise, with its superior per-
formance across multiple metrics. The hybrid model using K-
means and the isolation forest showed an accuracy of 0.95,
and the other hybrid model combining supervised and unsuper-
vised learning using K-Nearest Neighbor and the local outlier
factor showed an accuracy of 0.80 and the two Variational
Autoencoder models showed an average accuracy of 0.69 at
the beginning and going up to 0.72 after incorporating Transfer
Learning.
Another critical constraint is the context dependence of
petroleum distribution anomalies. All anomalies picked up
may not indicate faults or fraud; some deviations could be
due to operational constraints, such as emergency redirection,
fluctuating demand, or regulatory adjustments. Traditional
machine learning models lack context awareness and hence
cannot distinguish between genuinely alarming anomalies and
permissible deviations, leading to potential false alarms. This
could produce undesirable intervention during operations or
causative delays. To negate this, the incorporation of future
work could use external context knowledge such as weather,
real-time logistic constraints, or domain knowledge regula-
tions to strengthen anomaly interpretation and suppress false
alarms.Additionally, hybrid approaches combining machine
learning with expert-driven anomaly validation could enhance
decision-making reliability.
This suggests that ensemble methods, which combine the
strengths of multiple decision trees, are particularly effective
for this type of problem. However, through this study we
were also faced with limitations. The relatively small size
of the dataset probably restricted the ability of our models
to fully generalize, particularly for more complex scenarios.
This limitation suggests that increasing the amount of data,
particularly with more examples of faulty instances, could
improve model performance.
